1:
"For yourselves, brethren, know our entrance in unto you, that it was not in vain:" The brief verse notes Paul's appeal that his ministry among them was not in vain. The grammatical voices note the appeal to their knowledge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul turns to defend and describe his ministry, appealing to their own memory: "yourselves, brethren, know our entrance in (our coming, our visit) unto you" — they themselves could testify to the character of his work among them.

On "that it was not in vain," the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Barnes and Calvin note the meaning: his entrance "was not in vain (kene — empty, fruitless, void of result)" — not hollow or powerless, but full of effect; his ministry had borne real fruit in their conversion, as the previous chapter showed.

The grammatical-historical note the defence: against any who might disparage Paul, he points to the manifest result — the very existence of the church proved his coming was not empty. The verse displays Paul's appeal that his ministry was not in vain.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul appeals to their own knowledge — "yourselves know our entrance in (our visit) unto you," they could testify to his work (Barnes, Robertson); "that it was not in vain (empty, fruitless)," not hollow but full of effect, bearing real fruit (Barnes, Calvin); the existence of the church proving his coming not empty (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fruitful ministry. The verse marks the appeal: yourselves know. The verse displays the entrance: among them. The verse displays the fruit: not in vain. The verse displays the proof: the church itself. The appeal displays Paul's ministry shown fruitful by its result. The verse displays the entrance: known. The verse displays the work: effective. The verse displays the vanity: denied. Paul's appeal that his ministry among them was not in vain displays his confident defence: he calls the Thessalonians themselves to witness that his coming among them was no empty or fruitless thing — the manifest result, their own conversion, proved its power. The verse displays the fruitful entrance, made amid suffering and boldness (v.2).
2:
"But even after that we had suffered before, and were shamefully entreated, as ye know, at Philippi, we were bold in our God to speak unto you the gospel of God with much contention." The witnesses note Paul's boldness to preach despite suffering at Philippi. On "after that we had suffered before, and were shamefully entreated... at Philippi," the grammatical voices note the prior suffering. Barnes and Robertson note Paul recalls the ill-treatment that preceded his coming: "we had suffered before, and were shamefully entreated (insulted, outraged) at Philippi" — the beating and imprisonment there (Acts 16:22-24), a public indignity to Roman citizens.

On "we were bold in our God to speak unto you the gospel," the witnesses note the courage. Barnes and Calvin note that despite this, "we were bold (waxed bold, took courage) in our God to speak unto you the gospel of God" — not intimidated by recent suffering, Paul preached boldly at Thessalonica; his courage was "in our God," drawn from divine strength.

On "with much contention," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the struggle: he preached "with much contention (agoni — conflict, struggle, amid great opposition)" — his preaching was a strenuous contest against opposition and danger. The verse displays Paul's boldness to preach despite suffering.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul recalls the prior ill-treatment — "suffered before, and shamefully entreated (insulted) at Philippi," the beating and imprisonment there (Acts 16, Barnes, Robertson); yet "we were bold (took courage) in our God to speak the gospel," undeterred, his courage drawn from God (Barnes, Calvin); "with much contention (conflict, struggle)," preaching amid great opposition (Wesley). The verse displays bold preaching. The verse marks the suffering: at Philippi. The verse displays the courage: bold in our God. The verse displays the message: the gospel of God. The verse displays the struggle: much contention. The boldness displays Paul preaching despite suffering and opposition. The verse displays the Philippi: suffered. The verse displays the boldness: in God. The verse displays the contention: much. Paul's boldness to preach despite suffering at Philippi displays the courage of his ministry: though recently beaten and shamefully treated at Philippi, he came to Thessalonica and preached the gospel boldly — his courage drawn from God — amid much opposition and struggle. The verse displays the apostle's fearless ministry, sincere and unmixed with deceit (v.3).
3:
"For our exhortation was not of deceit, nor of uncleanness, nor in guile:" The brief verse notes the purity of Paul's preaching, free of deceit and guile. The grammatical voices note the threefold disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul defends the integrity of his message: "our exhortation (our preaching, appeal) was not of deceit (error, delusion — no false doctrine), nor of uncleanness (impure motive or aim), nor in guile (trickery, craftiness)" — his preaching was sound in doctrine, pure in motive, and honest in method.

On "not of deceit," the witnesses note the truth. Barnes and Calvin note that "deceit" means error or delusion: Paul's message was no fable or falsehood but the truth of God; he preached no error.

On "nor of uncleanness, nor in guile," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the purity and honesty: his preaching sprang from no impure motive (such as greed or lust, common to the false teachers of the age) and used no guile or trickery to win followers. The verse displays the purity of Paul's preaching.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's threefold disclaimer — "our exhortation was not of deceit (error, no false doctrine), nor of uncleanness (impure motive), nor in guile (trickery)," sound in doctrine, pure in motive, honest in method (Barnes, Robertson); "deceit" meaning error, his message the truth not falsehood (Barnes, Calvin); no impure motive and no guile to win followers (Wesley). The verse displays pure preaching. The verse marks the doctrine: not of deceit. The verse displays the motive: not of uncleanness. The verse displays the method: not in guile. The verse displays the integrity: sound and honest. The disclaimer displays Paul's preaching pure in every respect. The verse displays the deceit: absent. The verse displays the uncleanness: absent. The verse displays the guile: absent. The purity of Paul's preaching, free of deceit and guile, displays the integrity of his ministry: his appeal sprang from no false doctrine, no impure motive, and no crafty trickery — sound in truth, pure in aim, and honest in method, unlike the false teachers of the age. The verse displays the apostle's sincerity, accountable to God who tries the heart (v.4).
4:
"But as we were allowed of God to be put in trust with the gospel, even so we speak; not as pleasing men, but God, which trieth our hearts." The witnesses note Paul preaching to please God, who tries the heart, not men. On "as we were allowed of God to be put in trust with the gospel," the grammatical voices note the stewardship. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's sense of trust: "as we were allowed (approved, tested and found fit) of God to be put in trust with (entrusted with) the gospel" — God had examined and approved him, committing the gospel to his charge as a sacred trust.

On "even so we speak; not as pleasing men, but God," the witnesses note the aim. Barnes and Calvin note that, holding such a trust, "even so we speak; not as pleasing men, but God" — Paul preached to please God, the one who entrusted him, not to win the favour of men; the steward answers to his Master, not to the crowd.

On "which trieth our hearts," the Reformed Calvin notes the searching God: he preaches to please "God, which trieth (tests, proves, examines) our hearts" — the God who searches the inmost motives; Paul's aim is the approval of the one who sees the heart, not the applause of men who see only the outward. The verse displays Paul preaching to please God, not men.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's sense of trust — "as we were allowed (approved, found fit) of God to be put in trust with the gospel," God committing the gospel to his charge (Barnes, Robertson); "even so we speak; not as pleasing men, but God," preaching to please the Master who entrusted him, not the crowd (Barnes, Calvin); "which trieth (tests) our hearts," the God who searches the inmost motives, whose approval Paul seeks (Calvin). The verse displays pleasing God. The verse marks the trust: put in trust with the gospel. The verse displays the aim: please God, not men. The verse displays the searching: trieth the heart. The verse displays the steward: answering to God. The aim displays Paul preaching for God's approval. The verse displays the trust: the gospel. The verse displays the pleasing: of God. The verse displays the heart: tried. Paul preaching to please God, who tries the heart, not men, displays the principle of his ministry: entrusted by God with the gospel as a sacred stewardship, he speaks to please the God who tested and approved him and who searches the heart — not to win the favour of men. The verse displays the apostle's God-ward aim, free from flattery and greed (vv.5-6).
5:
"For neither at any time used we flattering words, as ye know, nor a cloke of covetousness; God is witness:" The brief verse notes Paul's freedom from flattery and covetousness. The grammatical voices note the disclaimers. Barnes and Robertson note Paul disclaims two faults of the false teacher: "neither at any time used we flattering words (speech meant to please and win favour, as ye know)" — he never fawned or flattered to gain influence.

On "nor a cloke of covetousness," the witnesses note the hidden greed. Barnes and Calvin note the second: "nor a cloke (pretext, pretense, a covering) of covetousness" — he never used his ministry as a mask to conceal greed for gain; the preaching was no cover for avarice.

On the two witnesses, the Reformed Calvin notes the appeal: for the flattery, Paul appeals to the Thessalonians themselves ("as ye know"); for the hidden greed, which only God could see, he appeals to God ("God is witness") — the inward motive known to God alone. The verse displays Paul's freedom from flattery and covetousness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul disclaims two faults — "neither used we flattering words (speech to win favour), as ye know," never fawning to gain influence (Barnes, Robertson); "nor a cloke (pretext) of covetousness," never masking greed with ministry (Barnes, Calvin); appealing for the outward flattery to the Thessalonians ("as ye know") and for the hidden greed to God ("God is witness," Calvin). The verse displays freedom from flattery. The verse marks the first: no flattering words. The verse displays the second: no cloke of covetousness. The verse displays the witnesses: they and God. The verse displays the integrity: pure motive. The disclaimer displays Paul free of flattery and hidden greed. The verse displays the flattery: absent. The verse displays the covetousness: absent. The verse displays the witness: God. Paul's freedom from flattery and covetousness displays the purity of his motive: he never used fawning speech to win favour, nor cloaked greed for gain under his ministry — appealing for the one to the Thessalonians' own knowledge, and for the hidden other to God who alone sees the heart. The verse displays the apostle's pure motive, free also from the pursuit of glory (v.6).
6:
"Nor of men sought we glory, neither of you, nor yet of others, when we might have been burdensome, as the apostles of Christ." The brief verse notes Paul not seeking glory, though he might have claimed support. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer of ambition. Barnes and Robertson note the third disclaimer: "nor of men sought we glory (honour, praise), neither of you, nor yet of others" — Paul did not preach to gain reputation or applause from anyone, neither the Thessalonians nor others.

On "when we might have been burdensome, as the apostles of Christ," interpreters note the right foregone. Barnes and JFB note that "we might have been burdensome (claimed maintenance, asserted our weight/authority) as the apostles of Christ" — Paul had the right, as an apostle, to be supported by the churches and to claim honour, but he forewent it; "burdensome" may mean either claiming support or pressing his apostolic authority.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the self-denial: though entitled to honour and maintenance, Paul waived his rights for the gospel's sake, that he might be no charge or burden to them. The verse displays Paul not seeking glory, though he might have claimed support.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the third disclaimer — "nor of men sought we glory (honour, praise), neither of you nor others," not preaching for reputation (Barnes, Robertson); "we might have been burdensome (claimed maintenance or pressed our authority) as the apostles of Christ," having the right but foregoing it (Barnes, JFB); waiving his rights for the gospel's sake (Wesley). The verse displays not seeking glory. The verse marks the disclaimer: no glory of men. The verse displays the right: might have been burdensome. The verse displays the apostleship: entitled. The verse displays the self-denial: foregone. The disclaimer displays Paul renouncing honour and his rights. The verse displays the glory: not sought. The verse displays the burden: not pressed. The verse displays the right: waived. Paul not seeking glory, though he might have claimed support, displays the selflessness of his ministry: he sought honour and praise from no one, and though entitled as an apostle to maintenance and weight, he forewent his rights, that he might be no burden to them. The verse displays the apostle's self-denial, gentle as a nursing mother (v.7).
7:
"But we were gentle among you, even as a nurse cherisheth her children:" The witnesses note Paul's gentleness, like a nursing mother — and a textual variant. On "we were gentle among you," interpreters note a famous textual variant. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and the puritan Henry follow the received reading "we were gentle (epioi — mild, kind, tender)" — Paul, who might have used apostolic authority, behaved with mildness and tenderness. But the manuscripts also strongly support "we were babes (nepioi — little children)" — differing by a single letter (the preceding word ending in -n), read by the Vulgate and Ethiopic and many ancient witnesses; Gill notes this reading, that Paul was harmless, modest, and disinterested as a little child. The two readings differ by one letter and the evidence is famously divided.

On "even as a nurse cherisheth her children," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note the tender image: "as a nurse (trophos — a nursing mother) cherisheth (warms, fosters, tenderly nurtures) her children" — Paul cared for the Thessalonians with the warm, self-giving tenderness of a mother nursing her own babes.

The eastern note the affection: the apostle, far from lording it over them, nourished them as a mother her infant. The verse displays Paul's gentleness, like a nursing mother.

The witnesses agree the sense: whether "gentle" or "babes," Paul behaved among them not with authority or self-assertion but with the tender, self-giving care of a nursing mother who cherishes her own children — warming and fostering them (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry). And they note the textual crux: the received "gentle (epioi)" and the strongly-attested "babes (nepioi)" differ by a single letter; the Vulgate, Ethiopic, and many witnesses read "babes" (Gill, the grammatical-historical), the evidence famously divided. The verse displays the gentleness and the variant. The verse marks the reading: gentle or babes. The verse displays the manner: not authority but tenderness. The verse displays the figure: a nursing mother. The verse displays the care: cherishing. The gentleness displays Paul nurturing them as a mother her child. The verse displays the gentleness: shown. The verse displays the nurse: cherishing. The verse displays the children: nourished. Paul's gentleness, like a nursing mother, displays the tenderness of his ministry and a textual crux: among the Thessalonians he was not domineering but gentle — or, by the strongly-attested variant, became a babe — cherishing them as a nursing mother fosters her own children. The verse displays the apostle's maternal love, ready to impart his very soul (v.8).
8:
"So being affectionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, but also our own souls, because ye were dear unto us." The brief verse notes Paul's readiness to impart his very soul out of love. The grammatical voices note the yearning affection. Barnes and Robertson note the depth of Paul's love: "so being affectionately desirous of you (yearning over you, full of tender longing)" — his care for them was no cold duty but warm, yearning affection.

On "we were willing to have imparted... not the gospel only, but also our own souls," the witnesses note the self-giving. Barnes and Calvin note the measure of his love: he was willing "to have imparted (shared, given) unto you, not the gospel of God only, but also our own souls (our very selves, our lives)" — he would gladly have given not just the message but his own life for them; love that gives itself.

On "because ye were dear unto us," the grammatical-historical note the ground: all this "because ye were dear (beloved) unto us" — they had become so precious to him that he held nothing back, not even himself. The verse displays Paul's readiness to impart his very soul out of love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the depth of his love — "affectionately desirous of you (yearning over you)," warm, tender longing not cold duty (Barnes, Robertson); willing "to have imparted not the gospel only but also our own souls (our very selves)," giving not just the message but his life (Barnes, Calvin); "because ye were dear (beloved) unto us," they so precious he held nothing back (grammatical-historical). The verse displays self-giving love. The verse marks the affection: yearning over them. The verse displays the gift: the gospel and his soul. The verse displays the self-giving: his very life. The verse displays the ground: they were dear. The love displays Paul giving himself for them. The verse displays the yearning: affectionate. The verse displays the soul: imparted. The verse displays the dearness: theirs. Paul's readiness to impart his very soul out of love displays the depth of his pastoral affection: yearning over the Thessalonians, he was willing to give them not only the gospel but his own life — because they had become so dear to him that he held nothing back. The verse displays the apostle's self-giving love, joined to his toil among them (v.9).
9:
"For ye remember, brethren, our labour and travail: for labouring night and day, because we would not be chargeable unto any of you, we preached unto you the gospel of God." The witnesses note Paul's labour night and day so as not to burden them. On "ye remember, brethren, our labour and travail," the grammatical voices note the toil. Barnes and Robertson note Paul appeals to their memory of his hard work: "ye remember our labour (kopos — toil) and travail (mochthos — hardship, weariness)" — the two strong words for exhausting work; Paul laboured with his own hands (tentmaking, Acts 18:3) to support himself.

On "labouring night and day," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Chrysostom note that he worked "night and day" — by his trade through the day and perhaps preaching besides, sparing no effort; the toil was constant and wearying.

On "because we would not be chargeable unto any of you," the Reformed Calvin notes the motive: he laboured thus "because we would not be chargeable (a burden, a financial charge) unto any of you" — Paul supported himself precisely so that he might preach the gospel free of charge, putting no obstacle in their way. The verse displays Paul's labour night and day so as not to burden them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul appeals to their memory — "ye remember our labour (toil) and travail (hardship)," the strong words for exhausting work, Paul labouring with his hands (Barnes, Robertson); "night and day," constant and wearying toil (Barnes, Chrysostom); "because we would not be chargeable (a burden) unto any of you," supporting himself to preach free of charge (Calvin). The verse displays his self-supporting toil. The verse marks the labour: toil and travail. The verse displays the constancy: night and day. The verse displays the motive: not chargeable. The verse displays the aim: the gospel free. The labour displays Paul working to burden no one. The verse displays the toil: remembered. The verse displays the night and day: laboured. The verse displays the charge: avoided. Paul's labour night and day so as not to burden them displays the self-denial of his ministry: he toiled with his own hands, night and day, in exhausting work, that he might preach the gospel free of charge and be a burden to none among them. The verse displays the apostle's self-supporting labour, with a clear conscience before them and God (v.10).
10:
"Ye are witnesses, and God also, how holily and justly and unblameably we behaved ourselves among you that believe:" The brief verse notes the holiness and blamelessness of Paul's conduct among them. The grammatical voices note the appeal to witnesses. Barnes and Robertson note Paul calls both the Thessalonians and God to testify to his conduct: "ye are witnesses, and God also" — they could vouch for his outward behaviour, God for his inward integrity.

On "how holily and justly and unblameably," the witnesses note the threefold character. Barnes and Calvin note the three adverbs: "holily (hosios — toward God, in piety and devotion), justly (dikaios — toward men, in righteousness and fairness), and unblameably (amemptos — without fault, giving no just ground for accusation)" — his conduct was pure toward God, righteous toward men, and blameless before all.

On "among you that believe," the grammatical-historical note the audience: his behaviour was open before "you that believe" — the believers had watched his daily life and could confirm its blamelessness; his walk matched his preaching. The verse displays the holiness and blamelessness of Paul's conduct.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul calls both witnesses — "ye are witnesses, and God also," they for his outward behaviour, God for his inward integrity (Barnes, Robertson); the three adverbs "holily (toward God), justly (toward men), unblameably (without fault)," pure, righteous, and blameless (Barnes, Calvin); "among you that believe," his daily life open before them, his walk matching his preaching (grammatical-historical). The verse displays blameless conduct. The verse marks the witnesses: they and God. The verse displays the holiness: toward God. The verse displays the justice: toward men. The verse displays the blamelessness: without fault. The conduct displays Paul holy, just, and blameless. The verse displays the witnesses: called. The verse displays the holiness: shown. The verse displays the blamelessness: confirmed. The holiness and blamelessness of Paul's conduct among them display the integrity of his life: he calls both the Thessalonians and God to witness that he behaved holily toward God, justly toward men, and blamelessly before all — his walk confirming his preaching. The verse displays the apostle's blameless life, exercised in fatherly exhortation (vv.11-12).
11:
"As ye know how we exhorted and comforted and charged every one of you, as a father doth his children," The brief verse notes Paul's fatherly dealing with each of them. The grammatical voices note the paternal image. Barnes and Robertson note that, having likened himself to a nursing mother (v.7), Paul now takes the image of a father: "as a father doth his children" — his care was both maternal in tenderness and paternal in guidance and exhortation.

On "exhorted and comforted and charged," the witnesses note the threefold ministry. Barnes and Calvin note the three verbs of fatherly dealing: "exhorted (urged, encouraged to duty), comforted (consoled, cheered the discouraged), and charged (testified, solemnly enjoined)" — Paul exhorted to right conduct, comforted the fainthearted, and solemnly charged them, as a wise father variously deals with his children.

On "every one of you," the grammatical-historical note the individual care: he dealt thus with "every one of you" — not the crowd in mass, but each person individually, with personal, particular attention. The verse displays Paul's fatherly dealing with each of them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the paternal image — "as a father doth his children," his care paternal in guidance as it was maternal in tenderness (Barnes, Robertson); the three verbs "exhorted (urged to duty), comforted (consoled the discouraged), charged (solemnly enjoined)," dealing variously as a wise father (Barnes, Calvin); "every one of you," each person individually, with personal attention (grammatical-historical). The verse displays fatherly dealing. The verse marks the image: as a father. The verse displays the threefold: exhorted, comforted, charged. The verse displays the individuality: every one. The verse displays the care: personal. The dealing displays Paul fathering each believer. The verse displays the father: his image. The verse displays the exhorting: and comforting. The verse displays the each: individually. Paul's fatherly dealing with each of them displays the personal care of his ministry: like a father with his own children, he exhorted, comforted, and charged every single one of them individually — guiding, cheering, and solemnly urging each as their needs required. The verse displays the apostle's paternal care, aimed at a worthy walk (v.12).
12:
"That ye would walk worthy of God, who hath called you unto his kingdom and glory." The brief verse notes the aim of Paul's care: a walk worthy of God. The grammatical voices note the goal. Barnes and Robertson note the purpose of all his fatherly exhortation: "that ye would walk worthy of God" — that the Thessalonians might so live and conduct themselves as befits those who belong to God; a walk corresponding to their high calling.

On "walk worthy of God," the witnesses note the standard. Barnes and Calvin note the lofty measure: not merely worthy of men or of the gospel, but "worthy of God" himself — a life that matches the character and honour of the God who called them; the highest conceivable standard.

On "who hath called you unto his kingdom and glory," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the calling: God "hath called (invited, summoned) you unto his kingdom and glory" — the believer is called to share God's own kingdom and glory; this high destiny is the motive for a worthy walk. The verse displays the aim of Paul's care: a walk worthy of God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the purpose of his exhortation — "that ye would walk worthy of God," living as befits those who belong to God (Barnes, Robertson); the lofty standard "worthy of God" himself, matching his character and honour (Barnes, Calvin); "who hath called you unto his kingdom and glory," the believer called to share God's kingdom and glory, the motive for a worthy walk (Wesley). The verse displays the worthy walk. The verse marks the aim: walk worthy of God. The verse displays the standard: worthy of God himself. The verse displays the calling: to his kingdom and glory. The verse displays the motive: the high destiny. The aim displays believers called to live worthy of God. The verse displays the walk: worthy. The verse displays the calling: to glory. The verse displays the kingdom: God's. The aim of Paul's care, a walk worthy of God, displays the goal of all his fatherly labour: that the Thessalonians might conduct themselves in a manner worthy of the God who called them — called, as they were, to share his own kingdom and glory. The verse displays the worthy walk, the end of the apostle's ministry, received as the very word of God (v.13).
13:
"For this cause also thank we God without ceasing, because, when ye received the word of God which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God, which effectually worketh also in you that believe." The witnesses note the word received as the word of God, working effectually in them. On "ye received the word of God... not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God," the grammatical voices note the right reception. Barnes and Robertson note the cause of Paul's renewed thanksgiving: when the Thessalonians "received the word of God which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men (a mere human message) but as it is in truth, the word of God" — they recognized the gospel for what it truly is: not Paul's word but God's, and received it with the reverence due to God's own speech.

On "which effectually worketh also in you that believe," the witnesses note the power. Barnes and Calvin note the result: the word "effectually worketh (energeitai — works mightily, is operative, produces its effect) also in you that believe" — the word of God, so received, is no dead letter but a living, working power, producing its saving effects in believers.

The puritan Henry notes the mark: the proof that they received it as God's word is that it works in them; the divine word, truly received by faith, transforms the life. The verse displays the word received as the word of God, working effectually.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the right reception — they "received the word of God... not as the word of men but as it is in truth, the word of God," recognizing the gospel as God's own speech and receiving it with due reverence (Barnes, Robertson); "which effectually worketh (works mightily, is operative) in you that believe," the word a living, working power producing its saving effects (Barnes, Calvin); the proof of right reception being that it works in them (Henry). The verse displays the working word. The verse marks the reception: as the word of God. The verse displays the negative: not the word of men. The verse displays the power: effectually worketh. The verse displays the proof: works in believers. The word displays God's speech transforming those who receive it. The verse displays the word: of God. The verse displays the reception: by faith. The verse displays the working: mighty. The word received as the word of God, working effectually in them, displays the cause of Paul's thanksgiving: the Thessalonians received the gospel not as a human message but as the very word of God — and so received, it works mightily in them as a living power, transforming all who believe. The verse displays the effectual word, proven by their endurance of suffering (v.14).
14:
"For ye, brethren, became followers of the churches of God which in Judaea are in Christ Jesus: for ye also have suffered like things of your own countrymen, even as they have of the Jews:" The witnesses note the Thessalonians sharing the sufferings of the Judaean churches. On "ye became followers of the churches of God which in Judaea are in Christ Jesus," the grammatical voices note the imitation. Barnes and Robertson note that the Thessalonians "became followers (imitators) of the churches of God... in Judaea" — they came to resemble the first Jewish-Christian churches, not by copying them deliberately but by undergoing the same experience of persecution.

On "ye also have suffered like things of your own countrymen," the witnesses note the persecution. Barnes and Calvin note the parallel: "ye also have suffered like things of your own countrymen" — as the Judaean churches suffered from the Jews, so the Thessalonian believers suffered from their own fellow-citizens (Acts 17:5); persecution from one's own people marked both.

On "even as they have of the Jews," the grammatical-historical note the comparison: "even as they (the Judaean churches) have (suffered) of the Jews" — the suffering of the mother churches from their unbelieving countrymen is the pattern the Thessalonians now shared. The verse displays the Thessalonians sharing the sufferings of the Judaean churches.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Thessalonians "became followers (imitators) of the churches of God in Judaea," resembling them by undergoing the same persecution (Barnes, Robertson); "ye also have suffered like things of your own countrymen," as the Judaean churches suffered from the Jews, so they from their fellow-citizens (Barnes, Calvin); "even as they have of the Jews," the mother churches' suffering the pattern (grammatical-historical). The verse displays shared suffering. The verse marks the imitation: followers of the Judaean churches. The verse displays the suffering: like things. The verse displays the source: own countrymen. The verse displays the parallel: as the Jews. The suffering displays the Thessalonians sharing the lot of the first churches. The verse displays the churches: in Judaea. The verse displays the suffering: shared. The verse displays the countrymen: persecuting. The Thessalonians sharing the sufferings of the Judaean churches display the mark of their genuine faith: they became like the first Jewish-Christian churches, suffering persecution from their own countrymen just as those churches suffered from the unbelieving Jews. The verse displays the fellowship of suffering, leading to Paul's words on the Jews who persecute (vv.15-16).
15:
"Who both killed the Lord Jesus, and their own prophets, and have persecuted us; and they please not God, and are contrary to all men:" The witnesses note the indictment of those who killed the Lord and the prophets. On "who both killed the Lord Jesus, and their own prophets," the grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's solemn indictment of the persecuting Jews (the unbelieving leaders, not the nation as such): they "both killed the Lord Jesus, and their own prophets" — guilty of the supreme crime of slaying the Lord, and before that of killing the prophets God sent them (cf. Matthew 23:31, 37).

On "and have persecuted us," the witnesses note the continued opposition. Barnes and Calvin note that the same enmity now "persecuted us (drove us out, hunted the apostles)" — the hostility that killed Christ and the prophets continued against his messengers.

On "they please not God, and are contrary to all men," the grammatical-historical note the character: "they please not God (their conduct is displeasing to him) and are contrary (hostile, opposed) to all men" — by their fierce opposition to the gospel's offer to the Gentiles, they set themselves against the good of all mankind. The verse displays the indictment of those who killed the Lord and the prophets.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's indictment of the persecuting Jews — they "killed the Lord Jesus, and their own prophets," guilty of slaying the Lord and the prophets (Matthew 23:31, 37, Barnes, Robertson); "have persecuted us," the same enmity hunting the apostles (Barnes, Calvin); "they please not God, and are contrary to all men," opposing the gospel's offer to the Gentiles, set against all mankind's good (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the indictment. The verse marks the crime: killed the Lord and prophets. The verse displays the opposition: persecuted us. The verse displays the displeasure: please not God. The verse displays the enmity: contrary to all men. The indictment displays the persecutors guilty and hostile. The verse displays the Lord: killed. The verse displays the prophets: slain. The verse displays the persecution: continued. The indictment of those who killed the Lord and the prophets displays Paul's solemn charge against the persecuting Jews: they slew the Lord Jesus and the prophets before him, drove out the apostles, displease God, and by opposing the gospel's offer to the Gentiles set themselves against all men. The verse displays the persecutors' guilt, filling up their sins (v.16).
16:
"Forbidding us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved, to fill up their sins alway: for the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost." The witnesses note the filling up of their sins and the wrath come upon them. On "forbidding us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved," the grammatical voices note the climax of their opposition. Barnes and Robertson note the specific charge: the persecutors were "forbidding (hindering) us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved" — opposing the very preaching by which the Gentiles could be saved; this was the crowning sin, begrudging salvation to others.

On "to fill up their sins alway," the witnesses note the accumulation. Barnes and Calvin note the result: by this opposition they were "filling up (completing, bringing to the full measure) their sins alway" — adding sin to sin until the measure of their iniquity was full (cf. Matthew 23:32, Genesis 15:16).

On "the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost," interpreters note the judgment. Barnes and Calvin note that "the wrath (of God) is come upon them to the uttermost (eis telos — at last, completely, to the end)" — the divine judgment had overtaken them, whether already begun (the prophetic past) or pointing to the impending destruction of Jerusalem; "to the uttermost" meaning either fully, finally, or to the end. The verse displays the filling up of their sins and the wrath upon them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the crowning sin — "forbidding (hindering) us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved," begrudging salvation to others (Barnes, Robertson); "to fill up their sins alway," adding sin to sin until the measure was full (Matthew 23:32, Barnes, Calvin); "the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost (at last, completely, or to the end)," divine judgment overtaking them, pointing to Jerusalem's destruction (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays sin filled and wrath come. The verse marks the sin: forbidding the Gentiles. The verse displays the accumulation: filling up sins. The verse displays the judgment: wrath come. The verse displays the measure: to the uttermost. The judgment displays the persecutors' sin full and wrath fallen. The verse displays the forbidding: their crime. The verse displays the sins: filled. The verse displays the wrath: come. The filling up of their sins and the wrath come upon them display the end of the persecutors' opposition: by hindering the Gentiles' salvation they filled up the measure of their sins, and the wrath of God had come upon them to the uttermost — judgment overtaking them at last. The verse displays the judgment on the persecutors, closing Paul's account and turning to his longing to return (v.17).
17:
"But we, brethren, being taken from you for a short time in presence, not in heart, endeavoured the more abundantly to see your face with great desire." The witnesses note Paul's longing to see them again, parted in presence but not in heart. On "being taken from you for a short time in presence, not in heart," the grammatical voices note the parting. Barnes and Robertson note the strong word for the separation: "being taken from you (aporphanisthentes — orphaned, bereaved, torn away as a parent from children) for a short time (a season) in presence (in body, face to face), not in heart" — Paul felt his enforced absence as a bereavement, though his heart remained with them.

On "in presence, not in heart," the witnesses note the bond. Barnes and Henry note the tender distinction: the separation was only outward and brief — "in presence, not in heart" — for in love and affection Paul was never parted from them; bodily absence could not break the bond of the heart.

On "endeavoured the more abundantly to see your face with great desire," the grammatical-historical note the longing: he "endeavoured the more abundantly (made every effort, with redoubled eagerness) to see your face with great desire (intense longing)" — the very separation deepened his yearning to return. The verse displays Paul's longing to see them again.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the strong word for parting — "being taken from you (orphaned, torn away as a parent from children) for a short time in presence, not in heart," Paul feeling his absence as a bereavement though his heart stayed (Barnes, Robertson); the separation only outward and brief, never parting them in love (Barnes, Henry); he "endeavoured the more abundantly (with redoubled effort) to see your face with great desire," the separation deepening his yearning (grammatical-historical). The verse displays his longing. The verse marks the parting: taken away. The verse displays the bond: not in heart. The verse displays the brevity: a short time. The verse displays the desire: to see their face. The longing displays Paul yearning to return to them. The verse displays the absence: bodily. The verse displays the heart: united. The verse displays the desire: great. Paul's longing to see them again, parted in presence but not in heart, displays the depth of his attachment: torn from them as a parent from children, he felt the brief separation as a bereavement — yet was never parted in heart, and longed with redoubled desire to see their face once more. The verse displays the apostle's yearning, thwarted by Satan (v.18).
18:
"Wherefore we would have come unto you, even I Paul, once and again; but Satan hindered us." The brief verse notes Paul's thwarted attempts to come, hindered by Satan. The grammatical voices note the repeated effort. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's earnest desire and attempts: "we would have come unto you, even I Paul, once and again (more than once, repeatedly)" — he had tried more than once to return to Thessalonica; the singular "I Paul" stresses his own personal longing.

On "once and again," the witnesses note the persistence. Barnes and Calvin note that "once and again" means repeatedly — his attempts were not a single passing wish but renewed efforts to come back to them.

On "but Satan hindered us," the Reformed Calvin notes the obstacle: "but Satan hindered (enekopsen — blocked, impeded, cut off, as breaking up a road) us" — some obstacle, whether persecution, hostile machinations, or circumstances Paul attributes to Satan's opposition, prevented his return; the adversary blocks the work of the gospel. The verse displays Paul's thwarted attempts to come, hindered by Satan.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's repeated effort — "we would have come unto you, even I Paul, once and again (repeatedly)," trying more than once to return, the singular stressing his personal longing (Barnes, Robertson); "once and again" meaning renewed attempts, not a passing wish (Barnes, Calvin); "but Satan hindered (blocked, impeded) us," the adversary preventing his return (Calvin). The verse displays the thwarted attempts. The verse marks the desire: would have come. The verse displays the persistence: once and again. The verse displays the obstacle: Satan hindered. The verse displays the opposition: the adversary. The attempts display Paul blocked from returning by Satan. The verse displays the coming: desired. The verse displays the effort: repeated. The verse displays the hindrance: Satan. Paul's thwarted attempts to come, hindered by Satan, display the reality of the adversary's opposition: Paul tried more than once, with personal longing, to return to the Thessalonians — but Satan blocked his way, impeding the work of the gospel. The verse displays the hindered return, even as the Thessalonians remain his hope and joy (vv.19-20).
19:
"For what is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing? Are not even ye in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming?" The witnesses note the Thessalonians as Paul's hope, joy, and crown at Christ's coming. On "what is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing?", the grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's affectionate rhetorical question: what is his "hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing (kauchesis — boasting, the victor's wreath of which one boasts)?" — what does he most prize and glory in?

On "are not even ye," the witnesses note the answer. Barnes and Calvin note the warm reply: "are not even ye?" — the Thessalonians themselves are his hope, joy, and crown; his converts are the reward and glory of his ministry, the wreath he will present.

On "in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming," the eastern Cyril notes the day: this glorying is "in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming (parousia)" — at Christ's return, when Paul will present his converts before the Lord; they will be his joy and crown in that great day. The verse displays the Thessalonians as Paul's hope, joy, and crown.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's rhetorical question — "what is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing (the victor's wreath of which one boasts)?", what he most prizes (Barnes, Robertson); the answer "are not even ye?", the Thessalonians his hope, joy, and crown, the reward of his ministry (Barnes, Calvin); "in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming," at Christ's return, when Paul presents his converts (Cyril). The verse displays his crown. The verse marks the question: hope, joy, crown. The verse displays the answer: even ye. The verse displays the reward: his converts. The verse displays the day: Christ's coming. The crown displays the Thessalonians as Paul's glory at the parousia. The verse displays the hope: theirs. The verse displays the joy: in them. The verse displays the coming: of Christ. The Thessalonians as Paul's hope, joy, and crown at Christ's coming display the worth he sets on his converts: they are his hope, his joy, and his victor's wreath — the glory he will present in the presence of the Lord Jesus at his coming. The verse displays the apostle's joy in his converts, repeated for emphasis (v.20).
20:
"For ye are our glory and joy." The brief verse notes the Thessalonians as Paul's glory and joy. The grammatical voices note the emphatic repetition. Barnes and the witnesses note that Paul, having asked the question (v.19), now answers it plainly and emphatically: "for ye are our glory and joy" — the Thessalonians are, without question, the apostle's glory and his joy.

On "our glory," the witnesses note the honour. Barnes and Gill note that the converts are Paul's "glory" — his honour and boast; they are the visible fruit of his labour, the proof of his ministry, in which he glories.

On "and joy," the witnesses note the gladness. Barnes and Clarke note that they are also his "joy" — the source of his deepest gladness; their faith and steadfastness fill his heart with rejoicing, now and supremely at Christ's coming. The verse displays the Thessalonians as Paul's glory and joy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul answers his own question plainly — "for ye are our glory and joy," the Thessalonians without question his glory and joy (Barnes, the witnesses); "glory," his honour and boast, the fruit of his labour in which he glories (Barnes, Gill); "joy," the source of his deepest gladness, their faith filling his heart with rejoicing (Barnes, Clarke). The verse displays his glory and joy. The verse marks the answer: ye are. The verse displays the glory: his honour. The verse displays the joy: his gladness. The verse displays the emphasis: plainly stated. The statement displays the Thessalonians as Paul's glory and joy. The verse displays the glory: theirs. The verse displays the joy: in them. The verse displays the fruit: of his labour. The Thessalonians as Paul's glory and joy display the warmth with which he closes his appeal: emphatically he declares that they are his glory — his honour and boast — and his joy, the deepest gladness of his heart. The verse displays the apostle's joy in his converts, closing the chapter and leading to his sending of Timothy (ch.3). There is something deeply moving in the plainness of the avowal, for the great apostle, who counted all earthly distinctions but loss, here owns without reserve that these poor and persecuted converts in a Macedonian city were the very treasure of his heart; not his learning, nor his labours, nor any honour among men, but the living faith of those he had begotten in the gospel was the glory he would carry into the presence of his Lord, and the joy that even now lightened the weariness of his imprisonment and toil.
